seo.title: Building Docker & Containers with open-source tools
seo.description: A step-by-step implementation guide for Docker & Containers with open-source tools.

content.intro: This guide outlines the implementation of a production-ready, multi-stage Dockerfile designed for web applications. It focuses on minimizing image size, optimizing layer caching for CI/CD performance, and implementing non-root security principles.
prerequisites: A Node.js or similar web application source code
Basic understanding of Linux filesystem permissions

steps.title: Configure the Build Stage with Specific Base Images
steps.explanation: Start with a specific versioned image rather than 'latest' to ensure build reproducibility. Use the full-featured image for the build stage to provide necessary build tools (compilers, headers) that will be discarded in the final image.
pitfalls: Using 'latest' tags which can break builds when upstream images update unexpectedly.
Using Alpine for the build stage when dependencies require glibc (common in Python/Node native modules).

steps.title: Implement Layer Caching for Dependencies
steps.explanation: Copy only dependency manifests (package.json, lockfiles) before copying the entire source code. This ensures that the 'install' layer is only re-run when dependencies change, significantly speeding up CI/CD pipelines.
pitfalls: Copying source code before running install, which invalidates the cache on every small code change.
Using 'npm install' instead of 'npm ci', which can result in inconsistent dependency versions.

steps.title: Execute Build and Assets Compilation
steps.explanation: Copy the remaining source files and execute the build command. Since dependencies are already cached in the previous layer, this step only processes application logic.

steps.title: Create the Minimal Runtime Stage
steps.explanation: Initialize a new stage using a 'slim' or 'alpine' image. Copy only the necessary production artifacts (compiled code and production node_modules) from the build stage. This reduces the attack surface and image size by hundreds of megabytes.

code.content:
FROM node:20.11-bookworm-slim
WORKDIR /app
ENV NODE_ENV=production
COPY --from=build /app/dist ./dist
COPY --from=build /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY --from=build /app/package.json ./package.json

pitfalls: Forgetting to set NODE_ENV=production, which causes many frameworks to include dev-only overhead.
Copying the entire /app directory instead of specific build artifacts, re-introducing build-time bloat.

steps.title: Enforce Non-Root User Execution
steps.explanation: By default, Docker containers run as root. Explicitly switch to a low-privileged user provided by the base image (like 'node') and ensure permissions are correctly set for the application directory.

code.content:
RUN chown -R node:node /app
USER node
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["node", "dist/main.js"]

pitfalls: Setting USER before chown, which prevents the user from having permissions to the application files.
Binding to ports below 1024 (like 80) which requires root privileges; use high ports like 3000 instead.

steps.title: Optimize with .dockerignore
steps.explanation: Create a .dockerignore file to prevent local development artifacts, logs, and secrets from being sent to the Docker daemon during the build context transfer.
pitfalls: Including .git folders, which significantly increases build context size and can leak source history.
Including local node_modules which may contain binaries incompatible with the container OS.

content.conclusion: By following this multi-stage pattern, you achieve a secure, high-performance container image. The resulting image contains only runtime essentials, executes as a non-privileged user, and leverages Docker's layer caching for rapid deployment cycles.
